description: Snapshot-backed subset of the axis (b) gold set (evals/gold/status.yaml, 120 DOIs: 25 retracted / 25 corrected / 25 expression-of-concern / 45 current). Twelve of those DOIs, three per class, are recorded here so test_status.py can assert the classification offline. Two extra rows carry the trap that axis (b) exists to survive: the Lancet Surgisphere paper (retracted) and the Lancet RETRACTION NOTICE for that same paper, which is itself a perfectly current document. Both carry Crossref `update-to: [{type: retraction}]`, and reading that array naively would classify the notice as retracted.

cases.notes: Elsevier deposits update-to on the retracted article, pointing at itself.
A WITHDRAWN article. Withdrawal maps to the retracted verdict.
The notice DOI differs from the article DOI, so the update-to entry points elsewhere and must still be counted: this work is not a notice document.
Mehra et al. 2020, the Surgisphere hydroxychloroquine paper. Its update graph carries a retraction, a correction, and an expression of concern; the strongest wins.

THE TRAP. This is the RETRACTION NOTICE for the paper above. It carries update-to: [{type: retraction, DOI: <that paper>}] while being a current document itself. Classifying it as retracted would be exactly backwards, and a naive reader of the update-to array does precisely that.

The hardest row in the set, and it was found by running the classifier against the gold labels rather than by reasoning. This DOI is itself an expression-of-concern NOTICE (about 10.1016/s1474-4422(19)30140-1), and a separate expression of concern was later issued against the notice by a third DOI. So it is a notice document that acquired a status of its own. A rule that drops the whole update graph of a notice document classifies it 'current' and loses a real editorial notice; the pairing filter in _crossref_notices exists to keep it. An expression of concern is also a weaker signal than a retraction and is reported as its own verdict, never collapsed into retracted.
A 1973 paper: old enough that the absence of update metadata is the norm, and the verdict still has to be current rather than unchecked.
